content:
A honest look at why two developers switched hosting mid-build, what the workflow looks like now, and whether it was worth it.

5 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Most side projects don’t die because the code is too hard to write. They die because the idea never gets validated fast enough. The landing page takes too long. Deployment feels heavier than it should. By the time something is live, the excitement is already fading.
That’s exactly where we were with Render. The hosting worked fine — but the loop between idea, deployment, feedback, and iteration still felt slower and more expensive than a beginner-friendly side project needed. So we tried Hostinger Horizon. This is an honest account of what changed, what didn’t, and what we’d tell another developer considering the same move.
We moved TechLogStack’s own side project to Hostinger Horizon
on our own — nobody asked us to write this. This is just what
we found.

Why We Were Looking in the First Place
Render is a solid platform. This isn’t a takedown.
But for a project still finding its shape, every iteration on Render carried more weight than it needed to. A simple experiment started feeling like a commitment — setup, configuration, a sense that each new version needed more effort before it could go live and be seen by real users.
For that stage of a project, that’s a mismatch.
What we actually needed was speed, low cost, and a path from rough concept to something visible fast enough to test whether users cared at all. A few constraints made this clear:

• Budget was tight, so recurring platform cost mattered

• The project was still evolving, so flexibility mattered as much as features

• We needed SEO-ready output from day one — a site nobody finds doesn’t generate real validation

• We needed fast iteration cycles, because a prototype that takes too long to launch often dies before getting useful feedback
When your real job is answering “does this idea deserve more time?” — your tools should be optimized for answering that question quickly.

What Hostinger Horizon Actually Is
Horizon is Hostinger’s AI-assisted website builder with two tiers that matter for developers:
Starter — AI credits to generate a site quickly from a description. Good for going from zero to a visible first version without spending days on blank-page decisions.
Hobbyist — Adds a direct code editor inside the platform. The gap between builder convenience and development control closes significantly here.
The feature that changed our calculus most: exportable code. On either plan, you can export the generated output, run it locally, inspect the structure, modify styles, and keep learning through the process. That distinction matters more than it sounds. For a non-technical founder, a builder is valuable when it hides complexity. For a developer still learning, the better tool is one that accelerates the first draft without removing access to what’s underneath.

What the Workflow Actually Looks Like
The practical build loop we landed on:
Step 1 — Generate the baseline with AI credits Describe the project, let Horizon produce a working first version. The goal isn’t perfection — it’s having something concrete to react to. Critique is easier than invention. Improving an imperfect homepage is much faster than creating one from nothing.
Step 2 — Export and run locally Pull the code, run it locally, fix the things that need fixing. Spacing issues, layout inconsistencies, content that needs rewriting. This is where the learning happens — reading generated code is a surprisingly effective way to understand how things are structured.
Step 3 — Use the Hobbyist editor for mid-weight changes For changes bigger than a quick local fix but not big enough to warrant a full local build cycle, the in-platform code editor covers the gap cleanly.
Step 4 — Connect Search Console and Analytics immediately Not at launch. Immediately. Google Search Console shows whether pages are indexable and appearing in search. Google Analytics shows whether anyone is actually arriving and what they’re doing when they get there. For idea validation, this data is more valuable than any amount of internal polish.
The loop: generate → export → fix locally → deploy → read analytics → iterate. That cycle running fast is the whole point.

What’s Actually Better, What Isn’t
Better:

• Time from idea to live site is meaningfully shorter

• Cost pressure during the experimental phase is lower

• Exportable code keeps the project in developer hands — not locked in a closed builder

• SEO-ready output from the start, not retrofitted later
Not better:

• If your project already has a clear shape and you need standard deployment pipelines, CI/CD integrations, or more infrastructure control — Render (or similar) is still the better fit

• The AI generation quality depends heavily on how clearly you describe the project — vague prompts produce vague output

• Horizon is a starting point accelerator, not a replacement for actually understanding your own codebase
Honest summary: Horizon is the better tool for the earlier stage. Render is the better tool for the later stage. They’re not really competing for the same moment.

What We’d Tell Another Developer
The biggest mindset shift that made this move feel right: early-stage tools should be judged by how quickly they create evidence.
A side project doesn’t need the most powerful stack on day one. It needs a path to real user feedback. If a platform helps launch faster, reduces cost pressure during experimentation, and still leaves room for technical growth — that’s the right tool for that phase.
A few things that would have saved us time if we’d known them earlier:

• Separate idea validation from long-term architecture — the best platform for testing an idea is rarely the same platform for scaling it

• Use AI generation as a starting layer, not the final layer — export it, read it, improve it

• Don’t postpone SEO and analytics — search visibility is part of the experiment, not an optional extra

• Don’t feel guilty about prioritizing speed and affordability over architectural purity when you’re still in the “does this deserve to exist?” phase
The goal of the early stage is simple: get something real in front of real people, fast enough that you still have energy to respond to what you learn.
Horizon helped us do that. Whether it’ll help you depends entirely on what stage you’re actually in.

Quick Reference — Tools We Used
Hostinger Horizon — AI-assisted website builder with exportable code and in-platform editor on Hobbyist plan.
Render — Cloud hosting platform for web apps and APIs. Still our recommendation when the project shape is clearer and deployment pipelines matter more.
Google Search Console — Monitors indexing, search visibility, and crawl issues. Connect this on day one.
Google Analytics — Tracks real visitor behavior. The fastest way to replace opinion-driven iteration with evidence-driven iteration.
SEO (Search Engine Optimization) — The process of making pages findable and well-ranked in search. Part of validation, not a post-launch task.

The team behind TechLogStack, where we retell real production incidents from Netflix, GitHub, Stripe, and more in plain English. This post is a one-off experience share, separate from our usual engineering case studies.
